section_title: INTRODUCTION TO ES FUTURES REVIEW & BEARISH BREAKER DEFINITION
content:
This is the index Futures review, highlighting a very dynamic day. Attention is drawn to a specific down
- closed candle, its high, and a subsequent higher high. This pattern, from high, low, higher high, is identified
as a bearish breaker.

section_title: BREAKER AND IMBALANCE LEVELS
content:
- PERSONAL TRADING NOTES
The market action inside an imbalance and around the identified breaker is discussed. The speaker
explains why he did not call for a higher level than 39.69 today, attributing it to the breaker. He clarifies that
while the market could have gone higher, he was stopped out prematurely and had to step away from the
computers. He refers to his Twitter feed on January 9, 2023, for a minute-by-minute walkthrough of the
day's trades, noting that nothing was edited or deleted.

section_title: MEAN THRESHOLD AND CONSEQUENT ENCROACHMENT EXPLAINED
content:
The concept of a breaker is contrasted with the market moving into a mean threshold, which is defined as
half of an order block. Similarly, consequent encroachment is explained as half of an imbalance. Multiple
price points within the range of the imbalance and breaker are noted as approximately at the half-way mark.

section_title: HOURLY CHART ANALYSIS & NEAR-TERM OUTLOOK
content:
Review of the hourly chart levels provided earlier in the morning before market movements. Focus is on
how price reacts to a specific candle, which is identified as a potential order block. If price respects this
level, it's a bullish sign. If it fails, the mean threshold of that candle is the 'last line of the sand.' Failure to
hold above this mean threshold would likely lead to an attack on the sell-side. For a bullish sentiment to
return, price would need to get back above half of the wick of the last significant candle on a closing basis.

section_title: DAILY VOLUME IMBALANCE, FAIR VALUE GAPS & ALGORITHMIC REALITY
content:
Examination of the S&P hourly chart, showing a daily volume imbalance and a fair value gap, indicating an
institutional order financial flow. The market traded down into this gap, then rallied to predetermined levels
shared on Twitter. A new weekly gap opening is also identified for lower time frame analysis. Support was
found precisely at the top of the old daily volume imbalance. This is highlighted as an example of precise
algorithmic behavior, contrasting with the unreliability of traditional, diagonally drawn support and
resistance lines, which are stated to have no algorithmic respect.

section_title: FAIR VALUE GAP MECHANICS AND PREDICTABLE MARKET REACTIONS
content:
The anchor points for the day's levels are shown: high of 39.69 and a quarter, low of 39.59 and a half. The
high of one candle (39.15.5) and the low of another (39.69.25) are also noted. The market's movement is
described: trades down, comes back up, trades down in a single pass, stops, goes lower, leaving an
inefficiency. This inefficiency creates a fair value gap, specifically a sell-side imbalance, buy-side
inefficiency (SIBI). The market is expected to move back up into it, and this indeed occurred. The bodies of
the candles respecting the high of the fair value gap is described as a 'beautiful sight.' The speaker
dismisses concerns about his teaching making the markets change or stop working, advising to 'keep
trading until it stops working'.

section_title: FUTURE BEARISH PROJECTIONS & KEY LIQUIDITY TARGETS
content:
A concern is raised about the market losing the current fair value gap, which is the next one down between
a specific low and high, at equilibrium. Looking below equilibrium for a discount, another fair value gap is
identified. If price drops below this, there's a significant amount of liquidity (a 'ton of money') sitting around
38.18 to 30.00. The expectation is that if the current fair value gap gives way, price might go down, possibly
retest it as resistance, and then continue a rundown. A smaller fair value gap is noted for further analysis
on the 15-minute timeframe.

section_title: UTILIZING TWITTER FOR REAL-TIME MARKET ALERTS
content:
The speaker emphasizes the importance of following his Twitter feed for real-time alerts and market levels.
He notes that Twitter offers a translate function, making it accessible to non-English speakers. He uses
Twitter like a text message service during the New York session (roughly 8:30 AM to 10:00 AM) to provide
minute-by-minute updates until live streams begin in February. He addresses potential complaints about
notification frequency by suggesting users turn off notifications and review posts later, stating he blocks
those who complain about his free content.

section_title: HOURLY FAIR VALUE GAP AND CONSEQUENT ENCROACHMENT PRECISION
content:
The hourly SIBI (sell-side imbalance buy-side inefficiency) fair value gap is pointed out, along with the top
of the daily volume imbalance (orange shaded area) that served as an upside objective last week and
found support. The market rallied into the low end objective. The speaker clarifies his initial estimate of
39.59 in tweets, stating the precise consequent encroachment (mid-range of the hourly fair value gap) is
39.59 and three-quarters. He acknowledges that new traders might find this complex but assures them that
repetition builds understanding and it gets easier.

section_title: NOON REVERSAL & MARKET SELL-OFF DYNAMICS
content:
- WEEKLY HIGH EXPECTATION
Around noon, a market reversal occurred after reaching the top of the hourly fair value gap. A small gap led
to a beautiful sell-off. A SIBI was then traded back up into, leaving some of it open, which is typical for a
heavy and weak market. The market continued lower, into the daily volume imbalance and the new week
gap opening. The speaker concludes that the market had a 'beautiful breakdown' and 'crazy meltdown' on
S&P, suggesting that the high of the week was likely already established.

section_title: 5-MINUTE CHART EARLY MORNING BREAKDOWN
content:
Moving to the 5-minute chart, more details are presented for the morning session. Sell-side liquidity was
noted below relative equal lows. Around the 8:05 AM candle, with no news drivers, the advice was to relax
until the 9:30 AM opening. Attention was drawn to a breaker on the 1-minute and 5-minute charts,
specifically the range of the 7:35 AM candle, which was to be extended in time. The market was choppy,
taking out sell-side, then buy-side, before trading down to an order block. Consolidation then occurred
inside the breaker, as previously instructed to identify with a rectangle.

section_title: 9:30 OPEN DISORIENTATION & ES VS. US500 CHART COMPARISON
content:
At the 9:30 AM open, the market wicked down, took out sell-side, and then moved into an order block. The
speaker admits to becoming disoriented due to toggling between ES futures and the US500 CFD charts to
accommodate traders who use US500. He highlights an error where he mistook a new week opening gap
on the US500 chart for the ES chart, which has slightly different prices. This emphasizes the distinction
between the actual ES S&P 500 futures and the US500 CFD.

section_title: ONE-MINUTE CHART
content:
- BULLISH BREAKER & BREAD AND BUTTER SETUP
On the one-minute chart, sell-side liquidity was taken, price came back above, then down to an order block
(around the 8:00 AM candle), finding support and consolidating. A rally higher was anticipated to reach a
short-term high, resulting in 10-12 handles, described as a 'bread and butter setup.' The bullish breaker
(low to high to lower low) range, identified earlier, was where the market wicked down at the 9:30 AM open,
took out sell-side, and then came back inside the breaker.

section_title: TRADING EXECUTION & THE LEARNING PROCESS
content:
The speaker switched focus back to 39.50. The market rallied into a fair value gap on the 9:45 AM candle,
engaging it before rallying further to 39.50 and then to 39.59, which is half of the hourly imbalance. He
acknowledges that new traders might find the concepts overwhelming but encourages persistence, stating
that 'repetition builds understanding' and it will get easier. He mentions plans for live annotation in February
to further demystify the process.

section_title: TRADE PROOF, ETHICAL EXITS & MARKET IMPACT MISCONCEPTIONS
content:
Proof of trades is shown with arrows indicating buying candles right above the 39.50.50 level. The speaker
explains that his exits are intentionally delayed after tweeting to avoid accusations of 'pumping and
dumping' (profiting from his followers' actions). He emphasizes that he calls trades before executing them,
making his entries sometimes slightly earlier or later than ideal. He dismisses the notion that his followers
could collectively 'push' the S&P market by 20-30 handles, calling it 'nonsense'.

section_title: ALGORITHMIC PRECISION, PUBLIC STOP LOSS & MARKET STRUCTURE SHIFT
content:
The speaker reaffirms his understanding of the market's algorithmic nature and its repeatability. He
explains exiting above relative equal highs at 39.56.5. A stop-loss was placed publicly right below the high
end of the fair value gap because he had to be away from screens. This public stop-loss was then 'tagged'
before the market rallied up to his expected high end objective of 39.69.25 and slightly above it. Relative
equal highs were formed, followed by a breakdown, a shift in market structure, another fair value gap, a
rally up, and then a sell-off. Consequent encroachment (half of the imbalance) failed to hold, leading to
accumulation of short positions and a further sell signal.

section_title: MARKET MAKER SELL MODEL & SMART MONEY ACCUMULATION STRATEGY
content:
The entire day's price action is identified as a 'Market Maker Sell Model.' This involves a consolidation
phase with sell-side liquidity below, followed by a rally up. The speaker highlights buying inside a breaker,
contrasting it with the 'infantile approach' of retail traders who wait for breakouts. He emphasizes that with
knowledge of directional bias and the next draw on liquidity, smart money accumulates inside the breaker,
anticipating the breakout, rather than reacting to it.

section_title: BULLISH BREAKER & FAIR VALUE GAP SYNERGY
content:
A fair value gap is identified between a candle's high and low, trading down to consequent encroachment
perfectly. Inside an up-closed candle, within the range of a bullish breaker (low to high to lower low), a fair
value gap appears. This combination, especially after taking out sell-side liquidity at the 9:30 AM opening
price, is called a 'unicorn'
- a very strong algorithmic entry pattern. The speaker expresses confidence in
such an entry, stating it's a 'launching pad' and price is not going to retrace significantly. Subsequent
imbalances allow for adding to positions. For the rest of the week, the bias favors shorts unless price
moves above half of today's daily wick.

section_title: ES FUTURES VS. US500 CFD
content:
- DISORIENTATION & MARKET FOCUS
The hourly chart for US500 is shown, explaining why the speaker got disoriented today. He states it's easy
to lose track when looking at multiple markets. He clarifies that in his live streams, he will focus on only one
market (ES futures), not spreading attention across Forex, bonds, crude oil, or gold. He will provide
markups for US500, but individuals will need to watch it themselves due to the potential for distraction. He
emphasizes he is not a 'superhero' and needs to stay focused to explain real-time events effectively.

section_title: CFD DISCREPANCIES & THE IMPERATIVE OF LIVE ES DATA
content:
The US500 CFD, while generally moving in concert with ES futures, does not provide identical price levels
or precision, particularly in entry points. The CFD went 'grossly out of line' with ES expectations. The
speaker recommends investing in live data for ES and NQ futures on TradingView (he has no affiliation) to
avoid frustration from skewed precision. He notes that Forex traders are 'spoiled' by free real-time data but
stresses that for futures, paying for data is necessary for accurate analysis, saving traders from constant
chart discrepancies.

section_title: STUDENT SUCCESS SPOTLIGHT:
content:
'PALADIN'
- A QUARTER MILLION IN WITHDRAWALS
The speaker introduces a student named 'Paladin,' who, without payment or specific coaching from ICT,
has successfully withdrawn over a quarter-million dollars through several prop firms. Paladin is highlighted
as the highest earner through funded account trading among ICT's known students. Many other successful
students choose to remain anonymous due to the polarizing nature of ICT's public persona and industry
negativity. Paladin was recently interviewed by The Funded Trader (no affiliation).

section_title: VALIDATION OF ICT CONCEPTS & MENTORSHIP IMPACT
content:
Paladin's achievement is presented as undeniable proof that ICT's concepts work and lead to real money,
refuting claims that the teachings are a scam or ineffective. The speaker expresses pride in Paladin's
efforts and his ability to ignore critics and negative narratives. He emphasizes that this success is not
'demo' or 'market replay' but real money withdrawn. ICT provides his teaching for free (Forex, stock index
futures, stocks), and people worldwide are using this information to make real money. He encourages
aspiring traders to 'roll your sleeves up, do the work, submit to the process and wait until the results come'
and not take advice from those who 'can't do what we're doing' or rely on ineffective retail methods. He
expresses his honor in playing a small part in Paladin's success and hopes he continues to do well, even if
it means diverging from traditional academic paths.
